meta.sourceOfTruth: This file feeds the dashboard island (src/components/Dashboard.jsx). The weekly task edits this file AND writes a new Markdown post in src/content/posts/. Do not hand-edit the dashboard component for data changes.

pipeline.name: NVIDIA Rubin (GB300 successor)
pipeline.note: Next-gen GPU announced at GTC 2025. Rubin Ultra pairs two Rubin dies with 50% more HBM than B200. Expected 2026-2027 delivery. NVLink 6.0 at ~5.6 TB/s.

pipeline.name: Tenstorrent Galaxy (server pod)
pipeline.note: Full server system with 32 Blackhole cards. Targeting hyperscaler and cloud inference deployments. Open-source TT-Metal stack differentiates from closed-compiler alternatives.

pipeline.name: Cerebras WSE-4
pipeline.note: Next-generation wafer-scale engine. Expected to move to TSMC 3nm and extend on-chip SRAM beyond 44GB. No official confirmation; treat as speculative.

pipeline.name: AWS Inferentia3
pipeline.note: Next-gen successor to Trainium2/Inferentia2. Expected focus on cost-per-token efficiency for Amazon Bedrock workloads. No official specs yet.

pipeline.name: Fractile chip (unnamed)
pipeline.note: UK startup's SRAM memory-compute fusion ASIC. $220M raised May 2026. Architecture eliminates off-chip DRAM entirely — 100x speed, 90% cost reduction claimed vs GPU. Talks with Anthropic.

usecases.q: I need the highest tokens-per-second for batch inference at scale
usecases.a: Cerebras WSE-3 or SambaNova SN50 for the absolute throughput ceiling. WSE-3 runs trillion-parameter models 6.7× faster than the best GPU cloud. SN50 is optimised for agentic workloads with high-throughput continuous batching. NVIDIA B200 NVL72 is the safe GA choice.

usecases.q: I need the lowest latency for real-time / interactive applications
usecases.a: Groq LPU (now part of NVIDIA) is the benchmark for deterministic low-latency inference — no stalls, predictable execution. Cerebras WSE-3 also excels here due to massive on-chip SRAM eliminating off-chip fetches.

usecases.q: I'm running open models and want to avoid vendor lock-in
usecases.a: Tenstorrent Blackhole is the most open option — RISC-V cores, open-source TT-Metal SDK, open IP licensing. NVIDIA H100/B200 with vLLM/SGLang gives the richest ecosystem but CUDA is a de facto lock-in.

usecases.q: I need inference at the edge or on-device
usecases.a: Axelera Metis for edge AI deployment. For laptop/desktop, NVIDIA consumer GPUs (RTX 4090/5090) or Apple Silicon M-series are more practical than datacenter ASICs.

usecases.q: I'm a cloud-first shop and want managed inference
usecases.a: Google Trillium (TPU v6) on Vertex AI or AWS Trainium2 on SageMaker. Both are cloud-only but deeply integrated with their respective ecosystems and priced competitively for sustained workloads.

usecases.q: I want the lowest cost-per-token for a large deployment
usecases.a: Depends on model size. For frontier-scale (100B+): Cerebras or SambaNova. For mid-size (7–70B): NVIDIA H100 with quantisation and efficient batching (vLLM). Watch Fractile for the 90% cost-reduction promise in 2027.

watchlist.name: Luminous Computing
watchlist.note: Photonic computing startup targeting 100x performance per watt vs electronic ASICs for AI. Optical computing for matrix multiplication. Very early-stage.
watchlist.why: If optical interconnects pan out, the memory bandwidth problem dissolves — photonic in-memory compute could leapfrog all current architectures.

watchlist.name: Rain AI (Neuromorphic)
watchlist.note: In-memory neuromorphic ASIC targeting analog compute for sparse activations. Very different paradigm from current digital ASICs.
watchlist.why: Analog/neuromorphic compute could offer 1000x efficiency for certain workloads if it proves programmable enough for transformer architectures.

watchlist.name: Intel Gaudi 3
watchlist.note: Intel's latest AI accelerator. 128GB HBM2e at 3.7 TB/s, 1835 TFLOPS BF16 (sparse). Strong on multi-chip scaling with RoCE networking, avoiding NVLink.
watchlist.why: SambaNova-Intel partnership (Apr 2026) could turn Gaudi 3 into a serious inference platform with the right software stack. Worth watching the collaboration.

watchlist.name: Qualcomm Cloud AI 100 Ultra
watchlist.note: LPDDR5x-based inference chip with 128GB memory and 100W TDP. Targets cost-sensitive inference deployments.
watchlist.why: Extremely power-efficient design (100W vs 700W H100). If the ecosystem matures, the TCO story is compelling for medium-scale inference.

chips.name: NVIDIA H100 SXM5
chips.tagline: The incumbent workhorse — CUDA ecosystem moat and the broadest software support in AI.
chips.perfNote: H100 is the defining baseline for LLM inference in 2025-2026. Its CUDA ecosystem, rich software support (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM), and broad availability make it the default enterprise choice despite newer alternatives. The memory-bandwidth-to-compute ratio is tuned well for decode-phase LLM inference.
keyInnovations: Transformer Engine with FP8 mixed precision — first GPU with hardware-accelerated FP8
NVLink 4 at 900 GB/s for tight multi-GPU coupling
HBM3 at 3.35 TB/s — highest bandwidth per GPU at release
Confidential Computing with hardware TEE for secure AI inference
strengths: Broadest ecosystem: CUDA, PyTorch, vLLM, TensorRT-LLM all first-class
Proved at scale — millions deployed across hyperscalers
Flexible: training, fine-tuning, and inference on one chip
Strong used/spot market drives down effective cost
tradeoffs: Memory wall: 80GB HBM3 limits single-GPU model size
Inference is 'good enough' not optimal — GPU was designed for training too
Power hungry at 700W SXM5
CUDA dependency is a de facto moat and lock-in risk
bestUseCases: Production LLM inference where ecosystem and tooling matter most
Mixed training + inference workloads
Any shop already on CUDA/PyTorch
Fine-tuning and continued pre-training alongside inference
recent: H100 NVL (dual-GPU module) widely deployed in public clouds
Spot pricing compressed significantly as B200 availability grows
vLLM and SGLang achieves near-theoretical bandwidth utilisation on H100

chips.name: NVIDIA B200 / GB200
chips.tagline: Blackwell redefines the GPU ceiling — 4.5 PFLOPS FP4 and 8 TB/s memory bandwidth per chip.
chips.perfNote: B200 is NVIDIA's answer to the growing ASIC challenge — 5× inference throughput vs H100 for LLMs, 2.5× raw compute over H100. The NVL72 rack system (72 GPUs, 13.8 TB total HBM) can hold the largest frontier models in fast memory. FP4 is a new low-precision format hardware-accelerated for inference, meaningfully compressing model weights without accuracy loss at scale.
keyInnovations: FP4 Tensor Cores — first hardware FP4 support, halving model memory footprint vs FP8
NVLink 5.0 at 1.8 TB/s chip-to-chip vs 900 GB/s on H100
Dual-die design with NVLink Chip-to-Chip — scales to 192 GB per module
NVL72 rack: 72 GPUs share a single flat NVLink memory space
strengths: Highest raw performance per GPU of any shipping product
192 GB HBM3e per module fits 70B models with headroom
FP4 delivers near-FP8 quality at half the memory bandwidth
Full CUDA compatibility — all H100 software runs day-one
tradeoffs: 1000W TDP demands significant cooling infrastructure
Supply constraints — NVL72 systems on long wait lists
FP4 requires revalidation of model quality
Extremely expensive ($30K–$40K+ per GPU at launch)
bestUseCases: Frontier model serving (70B–405B parameters)
Highest throughput batch inference
Enterprises wanting maximum GPU performance with minimal software migration
Multi-modal inference at scale
recent: NVL72 rack systems (72× B200) shipping to hyperscalers from Q1 2026
GB200 NVL2 (CPU + 2× GPU module) available for dense edge inference
TensorRT-LLM gains FP4 support for B200 in Jan 2026

chips.name: AMD Instinct MI325X
chips.tagline: 288 GB HBM3e makes it the memory-capacity leader among GPUs — critical for serving massive models.
chips.perfNote: MI325X wins on memory capacity — 288 GB is nearly 4× an H100 and 50% more than a B200. This is decisive for serving very large models without tensor parallelism across many GPUs. The ROCm software stack has matured significantly in 2025-2026, with most major inference engines (vLLM, SGLang) adding first-class ROCm support. Performance vs NVIDIA is competitive on compute but the CUDA ecosystem gap persists.
keyInnovations: 288 GB HBM3e — largest memory capacity of any shipping GPU (as of H1 2026)
Infinity Fabric 3 at 896 GB/s for multi-GPU scaling
ROCm 6 with HIP for CUDA portability
FP8 Tensor Cores for efficient inference
strengths: Best single-GPU memory capacity — fits 70B models in FP16 on one chip
Lower price-to-memory-capacity ratio vs NVIDIA
ROCm ecosystem maturing rapidly
Open driver stack vs CUDA's proprietary model
tradeoffs: ROCm ecosystem still trails CUDA — some libraries unsupported or slower
Raw TFLOPS trails B200 significantly
Bandwidth (5.3 TB/s) lower than B200 (8.0 TB/s)
Fewer cloud deployment options vs NVIDIA
bestUseCases: Very large model inference where memory capacity trumps throughput
Teams willing to invest in ROCm optimization for lower hardware cost
Multi-modal models with large KV caches
Cost-sensitive inference at scale
recent: MI350X announced (MI325X successor, TSMC 3nm, expected late 2026)
ROCm 6.2 adds vLLM and SGLang first-class support
Meta and Google deploy MI300X/MI325X clusters alongside NVIDIA

chips.name: Cerebras WSE-3
chips.tagline: The biggest chip ever made — an entire silicon wafer as one die, eliminating inter-chip data movement.
chips.perfNote: WSE-3 shatters conventional chip design by eliminating reticle stitching boundaries. Its 44 GB of on-chip SRAM delivers 21 PB/s of bandwidth — about 2,625× more than HBM3e on a single H100. For LLM inference this is transformative: weights live on-chip and feed compute units without ever crossing slow off-chip memory channels. Demonstrated 6.7× speed advantage over the best GPU cloud on a 1T-parameter model. Cerebras IPO'd in late 2024.
keyInnovations: Wafer-Scale Integration (WSI): full 300mm wafer as single chip — no reticle limits
44 GB on-chip SRAM vs ~80 MB on a typical GPU — 550× more
21 PB/s on-chip bandwidth eliminates the GPU memory wall entirely
Swappable Memory System (SMS) for multi-chip model sharding beyond 44 GB
strengths: Unprecedented on-chip bandwidth — no off-chip bottleneck for weights
Best-in-class latency for autoregressive decode (data is always on-chip)
Linear scaling for batch inference — no NVLink-style coordination overhead
Purpose-built for inference: weights fit in on-chip SRAM for most models up to ~70B FP16
tradeoffs: 44 GB on-chip SRAM cannot hold the weights of 400B+ models alone without SMS
Inflexible: cannot train on this hardware alone (cluster configs required)
Availability limited: only via Cerebras cloud (condor-galaxy clusters) or private deployment
High system cost — requires full CS-3 system, not a card
bestUseCases: Ultra-low-latency inference for interactive applications
Trillion-parameter model serving at high throughput
Research and inference workloads where speed is the primary metric
Financial, healthcare, or agentic workloads requiring real-time token generation
recent: Cerebras IPO (CBRS) completed Oct 2024; stock volatile but company financially stable
Condor Galaxy 3 cluster (9× CS-3 systems) deployed with Abu Dhabi's G42
WSE-3 demonstrated 21,000 tokens/sec on Llama 3 405B — fastest ever reported
Partnership with Mayo Clinic for medical AI inference

chips.name: Groq LPU (Groq 3)
chips.tagline: Deterministic dataflow architecture: no scheduler stalls, zero memory surprises — lowest latency in the market.
chips.perfNote: Groq's LPU is architected on a radical premise: eliminate all runtime scheduling. In a GPU, the scheduler decides at runtime which threads run next — a source of unpredictable latency. Groq's compiler does all scheduling at compile time, producing a deterministic binary that runs in exactly the same time every invocation. The result: sub-millisecond first-token latency and jitter-free continuous generation. NVIDIA's $20B acquisition in Dec 2025 validates the architecture's value — Groq 3 was unveiled at GTC 2026 and integrates into NVIDIA's inference stack.
keyInnovations: Compile-time scheduling: the compiler assigns every instruction to every cycle — zero runtime stalls
Massive on-chip SRAM (~230 MB) as primary weight storage — weights never leave the chip during inference
Dataflow execution: data flows through the chip like a pipeline, not dispatched by a runtime scheduler
Deterministic latency: same model = same latency, every time — critical for SLA-constrained applications
strengths: Lowest first-token latency of any shipping inference hardware
Jitter-free — latency variance is near zero (predictable SLAs)
Simple deployment via GroqCloud API
Now backed by NVIDIA resources for next-gen development
tradeoffs: NVIDIA acquisition uncertainty — future product roadmap unclear
Limited model flexibility: GroqFlow compiler must support the architecture
No fine-tuning support — inference-only
On-chip SRAM limits single-chip model size (requires multi-chip for >13B)
bestUseCases: Real-time voice and conversational AI (latency-critical)
High-frequency trading or financial decisioning with AI
Any application where P99 latency consistency is non-negotiable
Low-latency agentic loops with many sequential LLM calls
recent: NVIDIA acquires Groq for $20B (Dec 2025) — largest ever AI chip startup acquisition
Groq 3 LPU unveiled at NVIDIA GTC Mar 2026
GroqCloud reaches 1M registered developers
Integration with NVIDIA NIM inference platform announced

chips.name: SambaNova SN50 RDU
chips.tagline: Gen-5 Reconfigurable Dataflow Unit — purpose-built for agentic AI at scale, with Intel backing.
chips.perfNote: SambaNova's SN50 is the most recent major inference ASIC announcement, debuting Feb 2026 with a claimed 5× performance advantage over competitive chips. The Reconfigurable Dataflow Unit architecture — unlike a fixed GPU SIMT or a static LPU — adapts its dataflow graph at configuration time, allowing the same chip to efficiently run many different model architectures without recompilation. The three-tier memory hierarchy (SRAM → HBM → DRAM) is designed for agentic workloads that swap models and contexts rapidly.
keyInnovations: Reconfigurable Dataflow Unit: hardware dataflow graph that reconfigures per model at load time
Three-tier memory: on-chip SRAM + HBM for hot data + DRAM for model weights overflow
Designed for agentic AI: rapid context switching between models without re-loading weights
Intel collaboration for split-inference architecture (Intel CPUs + SN50 accelerator)
strengths: Reconfigurable: runs diverse model architectures without fixed-function limitations
Strong agentic workload profile — handles multi-step reasoning with rapid model swap
Intel partnership adds distribution muscle and server integration
$350M+ war chest ensures continued R&D and support
tradeoffs: Not yet shipping (H2 2026) — all performance claims are pre-GA
Software stack less mature than NVIDIA's ecosystem
Reconfigurability adds complexity vs Etched-style pure-ASIC simplicity
Limited public benchmarks to validate 5× claim
bestUseCases: Agentic AI systems with multiple models and rapid context switching
Enterprise inference with heterogeneous model portfolios
High-throughput continuous batching for API providers
Workloads where model mix changes frequently
recent: SN50 announced Feb 2026 with $350M+ investment round
Intel collaboration announced Apr 2026 — Gaudi 3 + SN50 split architecture for inference
Customer preview programme open for H2 2026 delivery
SambaNova Cloud running SN40L (predecessor) demonstrating fastest public Llama-3 throughput

chips.name: Etched Sohu
chips.tagline: Hardwires the transformer into silicon — fastest possible for attention, but runs nothing else.

facts.v: Not publicly available as of Q2 2026

chips.perfNote: Etched's bet is the most extreme in the field: instead of building a programmable processor that runs transformer code, they built silicon that IS the transformer. The attention mechanism, MLP layers, and layer normalisation are not 'computed' — they are physical circuits on the chip. This eliminates all the overhead of a general-purpose compute pipeline: no instruction fetch, no scheduling, no cache misses. The claimed 20× throughput advantage over GPU baselines for the same workload class is plausible given this architecture. The catch: run anything other than a standard transformer and the chip is a paperweight.
keyInnovations: Attention hardwired into silicon: no programmatic attention computation, it's fixed-function circuits
MLP and normalisation layers also fixed — all transformer math burned into the die
Eliminates instruction decode, scheduler, and general-purpose pipeline entirely
Smallest possible critical path: data flow is determined by circuit topology, not code
strengths: Highest possible throughput-per-watt for standard transformer inference
No software overhead: the model IS the hardware for the core compute
Simple deployment if your workload is 100% transformer-based
Power efficiency should be industry-leading
tradeoffs: Zero flexibility: SSMs (Mamba, Jamba), MoEs with unusual routing, custom attention variants not supported
Not publicly available — no independent benchmarks exist yet
Architecture risk: if attention evolves (e.g. linear attention at scale), chip is obsolete
Full NRE amortisation requires very large volumes
bestUseCases: High-volume transformer inference where the model architecture is frozen
API providers running a single flagship model (e.g. GPT-class) at massive scale
Any workload where transformer throughput is the only metric that matters
recent: Raised $70M Series A (2024) with Google, Andreessen Horowitz, Peter Thiel backing
Chip taped out on TSMC 3nm — production timeline still TBD
No public availability as of Q2 2026; waiting list for early access
Architecture debate: some researchers argue linear attention will outpace quadratic, threatening Sohu's fixed design

chips.name: Tenstorrent Blackhole
chips.tagline: Jim Keller's open-source RISC-V mesh — programmable, licensable, and the most developer-transparent chip in the field.
chips.metric: Up to 768 TFLOPS BF16 · RISC-V programmable mesh

facts.v: RISC-V Tensix tile mesh with NoC
Open IP — third parties can license and produce chips
Jim Keller (AMD K8, Apple A4/A5, Tesla FSD)

chips.perfNote: Tenstorrent's Blackhole is the most open chip in the inference ASIC race — RISC-V cores, Apache-licensed TT-Metal SDK, and an IP licensing model that lets other companies build their own Tensix-based chips. This is Jim Keller's vision of commoditising AI compute at the silicon level. Each Tensix tile is a complete RISC-V processor with local SRAM and dedicated matrix units; they're connected via a 2D mesh NoC (Network on Chip) that routes data without a central scheduler.
keyInnovations: Tensix tile: self-contained RISC-V + matrix unit + local SRAM — fully autonomous compute node
2D mesh NoC: tiles communicate directly without a central memory controller bottleneck
Open IP licensing: third parties (OEMs, governments, cloud cos) can license and produce chips
TT-Metal: open-source bare-metal kernel SDK giving full hardware visibility
strengths: Most open chip architecture: BSD-licensed SDK, GitHub-public, no secret compiler
IP licensing enables ecosystem of derivative chips
Good mid-range inference performance at lower cost than NVIDIA
Jim Keller's architectural pedigree — credibility in the hardware community
tradeoffs: Lower raw TFLOPS vs Cerebras, B200, or even H100 at the chip level
TT-Metal requires lower-level programming than PyTorch/CUDA for optimal performance
Limited memory capacity (32 GB LPDDR5 on n150) limits model size on single card
Smaller ecosystem than NVIDIA — fewer pre-optimised models
bestUseCases: Research teams wanting full hardware transparency and custom kernel development
Edge and on-prem inference where NVIDIA's TCO is prohibitive
Governments or organisations wanting silicon independence from US vendors
Developers contributing to open AI hardware ecosystem
recent: Blackhole n150 and n300 cards sampling to select partners (H1 2026)
Samsung and LG SDI invest in Tenstorrent for custom chip development
TT-Metal 0.50 adds transformer-optimised kernels competitive with CUDA
Tenstorrent Galaxy server system (32 Blackhole cards) in preview

chips.name: Fractile (unnamed chip)
chips.tagline: No DRAM, no HBM — pure on-chip SRAM memory-compute fusion. 100× speed, 90% lower cost (claimed).
chips.perfNote: Fractile's core insight: the LLM inference bottleneck is not computation, it's moving model weights from DRAM to the compute units. Every token generated requires loading the full model weight matrix from off-chip memory. Fractile's architecture eliminates off-chip memory entirely — weights live in massive on-chip SRAM arrays, co-located with compute. By fusing memory and compute on the same die at every layer, it removes the bandwidth tax that limits every GPU and conventional ASIC. The 100× speed claim and 90% cost reduction are extraordinary — independent validation awaited, but the underlying physics is sound.
keyInnovations: Memory-compute fusion: SRAM arrays and compute units on the same die, no off-chip hops
Eliminates DRAM and HBM entirely — the most radical approach to the memory wall
Dense SRAM allows extremely high bandwidth between storage and compute at low energy
Designed specifically for LLM inference decode phase — the most memory-bandwidth-bound operation in AI
strengths: Addresses the root cause of inference inefficiency: data movement energy
If claims hold, cost-per-token would be revolutionary — 90% below GPU
Anthropic interest (early talks) gives credibility to the architecture
Well-funded ($220M) with strong VC backing
tradeoffs: No shipping hardware — all claims are theoretical as of June 2026
SRAM is ~50× more expensive per bit than DRAM — die area will be enormous or model size constrained
2027 deployment date is optimistic for a new process node chip
No independent benchmarks — a 100× claim without silicon is a red flag to verify
bestUseCases: If delivered: highest priority for frontier model API providers (Anthropic, OpenAI)
Ultra-low-cost inference for high-volume consumer AI products
Any workload dominated by the memory-bandwidth-constrained decode phase
recent: $220M Series B closed May 2026 at ~$1B valuation
Anthropic in early talks for chip supply agreement (May 2026, Tom's Hardware)
UK investment: £100M into London/Bristol labs over 3 years
Architecture papers expected at Hot Chips 2026

chips.name: d-Matrix Corsair
chips.tagline: Compute lives inside the memory — digital in-memory compute slashes data movement by orders of magnitude.
chips.metric: 100+ GB DRAM with co-located compute · 5× efficiency vs GPU

facts.v: Compute where data lives, not where the GPU is

chips.perfNote: d-Matrix takes a different approach than Fractile's SRAM fusion — they use DRAM (cheaper, denser) as the substrate and place compute logic directly inside the DRAM die. This 'digital in-memory compute' (DIMC) architecture means weights are never moved: the matrix multiplication happens in-situ within the memory array. The practical result is dramatic reduction in memory-to-compute energy, which accounts for ~70% of total inference power on conventional hardware. Microsoft's investment suggests serious enterprise validation interest.
keyInnovations: Digital in-memory compute: MAC operations execute inside the DRAM cell arrays
Weight-stationary dataflow: weights loaded once, never moved during inference
Eliminates the memory-to-compute bus entirely — computation happens where data resides
128 GB capacity at DRAM density — much larger model footprint than SRAM-based alternatives
strengths: DRAM density gives 128 GB capacity — fits 70B models in FP16 without sharding
Dramatic energy reduction vs off-chip memory access
Microsoft investment = serious enterprise validation interest
DIMC approach is more manufacturable than exotic SRAM fusion
tradeoffs: DRAM bandwidth lower than SRAM — DIMC adds compute but doesn't fully close gap vs SRAM
Limited public benchmarks — sampling stage only
Novel manufacturing process: DRAM + logic on same die adds complexity
Software stack and toolchain still maturing
bestUseCases: Large model (70B+) inference where capacity and efficiency both matter
Enterprise inference with stable model architectures
Energy-sensitive datacentre deployments
recent: Corsair cards shipping to early customers (H1 2026)
Microsoft investment signals Azure inference qualification interest
d-Matrix joins Open Compute Project (OCP) — targeting rack-scale integration

chips.name: Google Trillium (TPU v6)
chips.tagline: Google's sixth-gen TPU — 4.7× ML compute vs TPUv4, available only inside Google Cloud.
chips.metric: 4.7× ML compute vs TPU v4 · HBM · Cloud-only

facts.v: Pods of 256 chips connected via ICI (Inter-Chip Interconnect)

chips.perfNote: Google's Trillium is the latest in a 10-year TPU lineage that has given Google a significant inference cost advantage inside its own products (Search, Bard/Gemini, Maps). The 4.7× improvement over TPU v4 is particularly impressive for HPC-class inference. ICI (Inter-Chip Interconnect) allows transparent scale-out to 256 chips per pod with near-linear efficiency. Not available standalone — you access it exclusively through Google Cloud.
keyInnovations: ICI (Inter-Chip Interconnect) at very high bandwidth — scales to 256-chip pods seamlessly
Matrix Unit redesigned for 4.7× throughput vs TPUv4
67% better energy efficiency through custom logic and process
Deep integration with JAX and XLA — Google's ML stack runs natively
strengths: Proven in Google's own Gemini inference at massive scale
4.7× TPU v4 compute per chip — generational leap
Well-priced for sustained workloads on Google Cloud
JAX/XLA ecosystem is excellent for research and production
tradeoffs: Cloud-only — no on-prem, no bring-your-own hardware
JAX/XLA expertise required for peak performance (less PyTorch-native than NVIDIA)
No access to chip specs or architecture whitepaper
Google Cloud vendor lock-in
bestUseCases: Gemini API workloads on Google Cloud
JAX-native research and production training + inference
Sustained enterprise inference on Google Cloud with SLA-backed pricing
Vertex AI Codey, Imagen, and other Google foundation model APIs
recent: Trillium (TPU v6e) GA on Google Cloud in Oct 2024
Gemini 1.5 and 2.0 inference runs primarily on Trillium
TPU v7 (Iron) rumoured for 2026 — targeting exascale training + inference

chips.name: AWS Trainium2 / Inferentia3
chips.tagline: Amazon's custom silicon for cost-efficient inference inside AWS — Inferentia3 targets lowest cost-per-token in Bedrock.
chips.perfNote: AWS has been quietly building its own silicon stack since 2018 (Graviton CPU, Nitro security, Inferentia/Trainium accelerators). Trainium2 is a serious upgrade — 3.7× throughput and 4× power efficiency over Trainium1, on TSMC 3nm. Amazon Bedrock uses Inferentia chips for many of its API inference endpoints, making AWS Bedrock's inference pricing partially subsidised by the TCO advantage of proprietary silicon. Not available outside AWS.
keyInnovations: NeuronLink: chip-to-chip interconnect at ~800 GB/s for multi-chip inference
FP8 support with custom number formats for inference compression
Trn2 UltraServer: 16 chips fully connected via NeuronLink in one instance
Neuron SDK provides compiled model caching across same-SKU instances
strengths: Bedrock integration: best-priced inference for models running natively on Inferentia
No ML expertise required — Bedrock abstracts the chip completely
3.7× cost efficiency vs previous gen improves AWS's margin and user pricing
Available today on-demand via ec2 trn2 instances
tradeoffs: AWS lock-in — cannot export models compiled with Neuron SDK to other clouds
Neuron SDK compilation required for optimal performance — PyTorch not plug-and-play
Limited model support list vs NVIDIA (only pre-validated architectures)
No visibility into chip specs — black box
bestUseCases: Amazon Bedrock API inference (Claude via Anthropic, Llama, Titan)
Cost-sensitive production inference already on AWS
AWS-native ML pipelines using SageMaker + Bedrock
Streaming inference workloads where Bedrock latency SLAs are acceptable
recent: Trainium2 Trn2 instances (trn2.48xlarge) GA on AWS in H2 2025
Anthropic Claude 3 Haiku available on Bedrock via Inferentia3 at lower cost
Amazon confirms Trainium3 in development (details under NDA)
Neuron SDK 2.20 adds Llama 3.1 405B support on Trn2 UltraServer
